name: Quebec
history: Quebec has a rich history dating back to French colonization in the early 17th century, when Samuel de Champlain established the city of Quebec City in 1608. The region became the heart of New France and remained predominantly French-speaking even after the British conquest in 1763, a uniqueness enshrined in the Quebec Act of 1774. Throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, Quebec developed a distinct national identity, culminating in the Quiet Revolution of the 1960s, which modernized the province and strengthened French-language protections. Today, Quebec stands as Canada's second-largest province by population and proudly preserves its French heritage and culture.
culturalNotes: Quebec is renowned for its vibrant French-Canadian culture, evident in its cuisine, festivals, music, and art that blend European traditions with North American influences. The province celebrates its identity through iconic events like the Montreal Jazz Festival and the Winter Carnival in Quebec City, while its neighborhoods feature colorful colonial architecture and lively street life. Quebec's distinct character is reinforced by strict language laws that protect French, making it the only primarily French-speaking region in North America.
bestTimeToVisit: Visit in fall (September-October) for foliage, or winter (December-February) for snow and festive atmosphere.
languageNotes: French is the official language of Quebec, and while English is widely understood in major cities, learning basic French phrases is respectful and appreciated. Useful expressions include "Bonjour" (hello), "Merci" (thank you), "Excusez-moi" (excuse me), and "Parlez-vous anglais?" (Do you speak English?).

places.name: Montreal
places.description: Canada's second-largest city and Quebec's cultural heart, featuring stunning architecture, world-class museums, and vibrant neighborhoods like the Plateau and Old Montreal. It's a bilingual metropolis with excellent food, nightlife, and artistic scenes that make it essential for any Quebec visit.

activities.detail: A stunning neo-Renaissance cathedral modeled after St. Peter's Basilica in Rome, featuring ornate interior decoration and impressive copper dome.
One of Canada's most visited museums with an extensive collection spanning decorative arts, contemporary works, and indigenous artifacts across multiple pavilions.
Explore cobblestone streets, historic stone buildings, boutiques, galleries, and restaurants in this charming waterfront district dating back to the 17th century.
An urban park offering hiking trails, panoramic city views from the summit, and seasonal activities with access to the iconic illuminated cross.
A vibrant public market in Little Italy featuring fresh produce, cheese, flowers, and prepared foods from local vendors year-round.
A trendy district known for colorful Victorian row houses with distinctive spiral staircases, independent boutiques, cafés, and street art.
An archaeology and history museum built on the site where Montreal was founded, with multimedia exhibits and preserved underground ruins.

food.detail: Hand-rolled, boiled in honey-sweetened water, and baked in wood-fired ovens, these denser and sweeter bagels from shops like St-Viateur Bagel are iconic.
The Quebec classic of crispy fries, cheese curds, and gravy that originated in rural Quebec and is found everywhere from food trucks to upscale restaurants.
A traditional Quebecois meat pie filled with ground pork, beef, and spices, typically served during holidays but available year-round at bakeries.
A spiced, cured beef brisket smoked and steamed, served lean on rye bread with mustard, best sampled at historic delis like Schwartz's.
A fried pastry dessert shaped like a beaver tail and topped with cinnamon sugar, chocolate, or other toppings, found at outdoor markets and cafés.

culture.detail: Montreal is officially French-speaking Quebec, so while English is spoken in tourist areas, learning basic French phrases and respecting the language is appreciated.
Locals take pride in Quebecois culture distinct from English Canada; you'll hear joual dialect and see cultural references that reflect this unique identity.
Dining out for brunch on Sunday is a cherished social ritual in Montreal, with restaurants offering lengthy menus and leisurely gathering spots.
Montreal hosts numerous festivals year-round (comedy, jazz, film, art), and attending or exploring festival sites is integral to the city's vibrant social calendar.

places.name: Quebec City
places.description: North America's most European city with preserved 17th-century architecture, charming cobblestone streets, and the iconic Château Frontenac dominating the skyline. The walled Old City is a UNESCO World Heritage Site that captures French colonial history and offers excellent cuisine, galleries, and cultural experiences.
imageQueries: Old Quebec City walls narrow streets

activities.detail: Visit this 84-meter waterfall located just outside the city, accessible by cable car or hiking trails with spectacular views of the St. Lawrence River.
Stroll along the only remaining fortified city walls in North America, encircling Vieux-Québec with panoramic views and historic plaques.
Ascend to this iconic castle hotel overlooking the old city and St. Lawrence River, offering guided tours and stunning photo opportunities.
Explore Quebec's history and culture through interactive exhibits in a modern museum in the Lower Old City near the waterfront.
Browse local artisan galleries, boutiques, and cafés on North America's oldest street, featuring narrow European-style architecture.
Cross the bridge to this charming island known for local farms, orchards, galleries, and traditional Québécois hospitality just minutes from downtown.

food.detail: A traditional French-Canadian meat pie filled with seasoned ground pork, beef, or veal, served as an appetizer or main course throughout the region.
Quebec's famous dish of crispy fries topped with fresh cheese curds and gravy, best enjoyed at casual bistros like Chez Ashton.
Sweet pastry shells filled with a gooey butter and brown sugar filling, a quintessential Canadian dessert found in local bakeries.
Communal melted cheese dining experiences popular in cozy restaurants throughout the old city, reflecting French Alpine traditions.
Local artisanal smoked salmon from nearby farms, available fresh at markets and specialty shops across the city.
A refined seafood pie featuring local fish and shellfish from the St. Lawrence River, served in upscale Québécois restaurants.

culture.detail: While English is spoken in tourist areas, learning basic French phrases shows respect and enhances your experience in this predominantly Francophone city.

culture.text: Winter is magical but requires preparation
culture.detail: Winters are cold and snowy; embrace ice skating, snowshoeing, and the Carnaval de Québec festival rather than avoiding the season.
Support independent galleries, studios, and boutiques owned by Québécois artists rather than chain stores to experience authentic local creativity.
Restaurants typically don't serve dinner until 6 PM, and locals dine around 7-8 PM, so plan meal times accordingly.

places.name: Laurentian Mountains
places.description: A stunning mountain range north of Montreal offering exceptional year-round outdoor recreation including skiing, hiking, and lake activities. Towns like Tremblant provide excellent infrastructure while preserving natural beauty and charming Quebec alpine village atmosphere.

activities.detail: Eastern Canada's largest ski resort with over 600 acres of terrain across two mountains, offering runs for all skill levels.
A scenic riverside hiking route following the Rivière Diable with waterfalls and forest views, accessible from multiple trailheads near Tremblant.
Paddle across the pristine alpine lake surrounded by forested mountains, with rental services and guided tours available in the village.

activities.text: Mountain biking the P'tit Train du Nord
activities.detail: A converted 200-kilometre rail trail spanning the entire region, perfect for cycling through villages, forests, and over historic trestle bridges.
Explore 3,000 square kilometres of boreal forest with opportunities for wildlife viewing, fishing, and backcountry camping.
Network of groomed and backcountry snowshoe trails wind through the mountains, offering peaceful winter exploration and occasional wildlife encounters.

food.detail: A traditional meat pie with pork, veal, and spices encased in pastry, served warm at local restaurants and bakeries throughout the region.
Quebec's signature dish of fries, gravy, and cheese curds appears throughout Tremblant with regional twists like smoked meat poutine.
During spring, hot maple syrup is poured onto clean snow, rolled on a wooden stick, and eaten—a quintessential Quebec treat available at sugar shacks in the region.
Montreal-style smoked meat and hand-rolled bagels, reflecting Montreal's culinary influence just south of the mountains, available at local delis.
Fresh trout and pike caught from regional lakes are featured at fine dining establishments, often prepared with herbs and local ingredients.

culture.detail: While many in Tremblant speak English, locals appreciate visitors making an effort with French, and menus often appear in French first.
Mont-Tremblant village is designed for walking with car-free cobblestone streets; explore on foot to fully experience the alpine village atmosphere and local shops.
The region values environmental preservation and peaceful co-existence; avoid excessive noise after 10 PM and stay on marked trails to protect wilderness areas.
The mountains experience distinct seasons with winter sports, spring sugar shack visits, summer hiking, and fall foliage; plan visits around these cycles for authentic experiences.

places.name: Mont-Tremblant
places.description: Eastern Canada's premier ski resort with a charming pedestrian village at its base, offering diverse winter sports and summer mountain activities. The resort village blends Quebecois architecture with modern amenities, making it ideal for families and outdoor enthusiasts.

activities.detail: Explore over 600 acres of terrain across two mountains with trails for all skill levels, from beginner runs to challenging black diamonds.
Wander the charming car-free village at the base featuring local shops, galleries, and restaurants in a picturesque Quebecois setting.
Ride the chairlift or hike up to panoramic ridge views of the Laurentian Mountains, especially stunning during fall foliage season.
Summer visitors can enjoy over 30 miles of trails ranging from beginner-friendly cross-country paths to technical downhill courses.
Cool off in the pristine lake with swimming, paddleboarding, and kayaking opportunities during the warmer months.
Explore over 50 kilometers of groomed trails through forests and across frozen lakes for a quieter winter experience.

food.detail: The iconic Quebec dish of crispy fries topped with cheese curds and hot gravy is a must-try comfort food found throughout the village.
A traditional French-Canadian meat pie filled with seasoned pork, beef, and spices that reflects the region's heritage and culinary traditions.
A beloved Quebec treat where hot maple syrup is poured onto clean snow, rolled on a stick, and eaten as a chewy candy.
Sweet pastry shells filled with a gooey mixture of butter, brown sugar, and eggs, a classic Canadian dessert available at local bakeries.
Regional restaurants serve locally-caught lake trout prepared simply to highlight the pristine flavors of Laurentian waters.

culture.detail: While English is spoken in the tourist village, French is the primary language in Mont-Tremblant; learning basic French phrases shows respect and enhances your experience.
Winter is peak season with crowds and higher prices, while summer and fall offer quieter experiences but limited snow activities; dress in layers year-round for mountain weather changes.
The resort emphasizes multi-generational activities and safety, with many venues offering childcare and kid-specific programs, reflecting Quebec's family-oriented values.
Montrealers and Quebecers treat Mont-Tremblant as their accessible wilderness escape; respecting the trails, environment, and local conservation efforts is important to the community.

places.name: Île d'Orléans
places.description: A picturesque island in the Saint Lawrence River near Quebec City, famous for its rural charm, historic farmhouses, and agricultural heritage including strawberry fields and cider producers. It offers a peaceful escape with scenic drives and authentic Quebec countryside culture.

activities.detail: Visit family-run strawberry farms during summer season (June-July) where you can pick your own fresh berries and purchase preserves and local products.
Drive or cycle the 67-kilometer circular route around the island that passes through all six parishes and showcases historic farmhouses, orchards, and Saint Lawrence River views.
Tour this working cidery to learn about traditional cider production and taste a variety of apple ciders in a restored heritage building.
Visit this beautifully preserved 17th-century church in the village of Sainte-Famille, featuring original wood interior and historical significance to French-Canadian settlement.
Explore this charming parish village with its boat launches, traditional homes, and waterfront access to the Saint Lawrence River.
Rent a bicycle to explore quiet rural roads lined with apple orchards, heritage farms, and pastoral landscapes at a leisurely pace.

food.detail: The island's signature crop, celebrated for their flavor and available fresh from farms, in jams, and as components of local desserts during summer months.

food.text: Homemade Apple Cider and Cider Donuts
food.detail: Sample fresh-pressed cider and warm cider donuts at local cideries and orchards, a traditional Quebec autumn experience.
This traditional French-Canadian spiced meat pie is prepared by local families and bakeries, reflecting authentic rural Quebec cuisine.
White and green asparagus grown locally in spring is sold at farm stands and featured in regional restaurants.
Local producers make house-style jams and preserves from island-grown berries and fruit, sold at farm shops and markets.

culture.detail: Time your visit for strawberry season (June-July) or apple harvest (September-October) when farms are most active and welcoming to visitors.
Remember this is a working agricultural community; farms are family businesses, so be courteous during visits and follow posted guidelines on private property.
The island maintains strong French-Canadian culture and traditions; learning basic French phrases shows respect, though English speakers are accommodated in tourist areas.
The island's charm lies in its rural pace; visitors are expected to drive slowly (under 60 km/h), stop frequently, and take time to appreciate the countryside rather than rushing through.

places.name: Montmorency Falls
places.description: A spectacular 83-meter waterfall just outside Quebec City, taller than Niagara Falls, accessible by hiking trails and a cable car offering stunning views. The surrounding park provides picnic areas and pathways that showcase Quebec's natural beauty near the provincial capital.

activities.detail: Take the aerial tramway 83 meters up the cliff face for panoramic views of the waterfall and the Saint Lawrence River below.
Walk the easy ground-level pathway that leads directly to the base of the falls for an immersive view of the cascade and mist.
Experience the thrilling 55-meter suspension bridge between the two cliff sides at the top of the falls for adrenaline and views.
Use the designated picnic areas throughout Parc de la Chute-Montmorency to enjoy a meal surrounded by natural woodland and waterfall vistas.
Visit multiple scenic viewpoints along the trails to capture the falls from different angles and elevations throughout the day.
During cold months, the waterfall freezes into a spectacular ice wall that attracts experienced climbers to the site.

food.detail: A traditional French-Canadian meat pie with pork filling that is quintessential comfort food found in local Quebec establishments near the park.
Quebec's iconic dish of crispy fries topped with gravy and squeaky cheese curds, available at park concessions and nearby restaurants.
A sweet pastry shell filled with gooey caramel and pecan filling that is a beloved Canadian dessert sold at the park's food vendors.
A classic French-Canadian dessert with a caramelized sugar crust found in local cafés near the falls entrance.

food.text: Maple Taffy on Snow (Tire sur la Neige)
food.detail: Hot maple syrup poured onto clean snow and rolled onto a stick, a traditional Quebec sweet treat especially popular in winter months.

culture.detail: Quebec is a French-speaking province, so most signage and staff communication will be in French; learning basic phrases is respectful and helpful.
The spray from the falls and Quebec's climate mean layered clothing and waterproof jackets are essential even on seemingly mild days.
Canadians typically queue patiently and respect line order; avoid cutting in line and be prepared for peak-time waits during summer and weekends.
Quebec parks emphasize environmental stewardship, so pack out all trash, stay on marked trails, and respect wildlife and vegetation along pathways.

places.name: Gaspésie Peninsula
places.description: A rugged and remote coastal peninsula famous for dramatic cliff formations, particularly Rocher Percé, and abundant marine wildlife including whales and seals. It offers spectacular scenic drives and authentic fishing village experiences for adventurous travelers seeking Quebec's raw natural beauty.

activities.detail: Hike to and around this iconic 433-meter sea stack with a natural arch, accessible by foot at low tide from Percé village.
Take boat tours from Gaspé or Percé to spot minke, fin, and beluga whales in their natural habitat during summer months.
Explore dramatic clifftop hiking trails with sweeping views of the Gulf of St. Lawrence and encounters with seals and seabirds.
Drive the famous loop through rugged coastal villages and fishing communities with stops at lookouts and small-town attractions.
Visit by boat from Percé to observe thousands of northern gannets and other seabirds on Canada's most accessible seabird colony.
Wander this authentic Acadian fishing village to experience traditional maritime culture and visit local artisan shops.

food.detail: Enjoy locally caught Atlantic lobster prepared simply grilled, boiled, or in traditional poutine at waterfront restaurants and fish shacks.
Sample crispy battered local cod and haddock served with hand-cut fries, a staple at seaside eateries throughout the peninsula.
Buy smoked salmon from local producers and fish markets, a traditional preservation method still practiced by Gaspésie fishermen.
Try Quebec's iconic dish elevated with fresh local seafood like shrimp, scallops, and fish instead of traditional gravy and curds.
Taste this traditional Acadian meat pie made with grated potatoes and chicken or pork, reflecting the region's Acadian heritage.

culture.detail: Plan your trip between June and September when roads are clear, wildlife is most active, and small villages are fully operational.
Many locals depend on commercial and recreational fishing; be respectful of active harbors, fishing equipment, and maritime culture.
Gaspésie has strong Acadian and Québécois heritage; understanding the region's deportation history and resilience enriches your visit.
The peninsula is remote with sudden weather changes and limited services in some areas; come prepared for self-sufficiency and unpredictability.

places.name: Rocher Percé
places.description: An iconic offshore rock formation with a distinctive natural arch, accessible by boat and from the beach at low tide, surrounded by seabirds and maritime history. It's one of Quebec's most photographed natural wonders and offers boat tours to Bonaventure Island for puffin viewing.

activities.detail: Circle the iconic pierced rock formation by boat to see the natural arch up close and photograph it from multiple angles.
Take a guided boat excursion to nearby Bonaventure Island to observe Atlantic puffins, razorbills, and other seabirds in their natural habitat during breeding season (May-September).
Walk across the exposed seafloor at low tide to reach Rocher Percé directly from the beach and explore tidal pools and marine life.
Learn about the region's Maritime heritage, Indigenous history, and geological formation at the local museum in Percé village.
Capture Rocher Percé during golden hour when the rock formation is beautifully illuminated and reflections appear on wet sand.
Rent a sea kayak to paddle around Rocher Percé and explore sea caves and coastal coves at water level.
Use binoculars from designated viewpoints along the beach to observe gannet colonies and migrating seabirds without a boat.

food.detail: Local seafood restaurants serve warm buttered lobster rolls made with Atlantic lobster caught in nearby waters.
Crispy battered local fish served with hand-cut fries, a staple at waterfront restaurants in Percé village.
Fresh cultivated mussels steamed in white wine and herbs, reflecting the Gaspésie region's maritime food traditions.
Local smoked salmon prepared using traditional Gaspé methods and available at village shops and restaurants.
Hearty soup combining local fish, mussels, and shrimp in a creamy or broth-based preparation.

culture.detail: Check tide schedules before visiting, as low tide occurs only twice daily and determines beach access and walking conditions around the rock.
Maintain distance from nesting sites on Bonaventure Island and avoid disturbing birds during breeding season; follow guide instructions carefully.
Coastal fog and wind conditions shift quickly in the Gaspésie region, so bring layers and waterproof gear even on sunny days.

places.name: Trois-Rivières
places.description: Quebec's third-largest city located between Montreal and Quebec City along the Saint Lawrence River, featuring a charming historic center and cultural attractions. It serves as a convenient stop on the route between the two major cities and offers local museums and riverside scenery.

activities.detail: A scenic riverside park offering walking trails, picnic areas, and views of the Saint Lawrence River from this island nature reserve.

activities.text: Musée québécois de la culture populaire
activities.detail: Housed in the historic Ursuline convent, this museum celebrates Quebec's folk traditions, crafts, and everyday cultural heritage through rotating exhibits.
Wander the charming historic streets lined with 18th and 19th-century stone buildings, boutiques, galleries, and restaurants in the city's original quarter.
A unique, quirky museum devoted to paper, printing, and industrial heritage housed in a restored paper mill building along the Saint-Maurice River.
Visit this historic chapel and museum to see religious artifacts and learn about the Ursuline Sisters' role in the city's founding.
A riverside boardwalk offering walking paths, public art installations, and scenic views of the Saint Lawrence River.

food.detail: A traditional Quebec meat pie filled with ground pork, beef, and spices, often served as a hearty comfort food in local restaurants.
A layered casserole of ground meat, corn, and mashed potatoes that is a beloved Quebec comfort dish and local favorite.
Fresh Atlantic snow crab, a regional specialty from the Saint Lawrence River, often boiled and served simply with melted butter.
A creamy fudge-like candy made from brown sugar and cream, a classic Quebec sweet treat sold in local shops and markets.
A rustic Québécois soup made with pearl barley, root vegetables, and sometimes meat, embodying the region's agricultural heritage.

culture.detail: While English is spoken in tourist areas, locals appreciate visitors attempting basic French phrases, and many menus and signs are in French first.

culture.text: Many shops close on Sundays or Monday mornings
culture.detail: Plan accordingly as some local boutiques and smaller restaurants observe traditional closing days that may differ from major cities.

culture.text: The city is between two major cities
culture.detail: Trois-Rivières works well as a half-day or overnight stop on the Montreal-to-Quebec City route rather than a multi-day destination.
The city embraces winter with seasonal events and activities along the frozen Saint Lawrence River, so visiting in colder months offers unique experiences.

places.name: Eastern Townships
places.description: A picturesque rural region southeast of Montreal known for rolling hills, charming villages, vineyards, and autumn foliage attracting visitors year-round. Towns like Sherbrooke and Magog offer cultural amenities, outdoor recreation, and quintessential Quebec pastoral beauty.

activities.detail: Take a scenic boat tour on the pristine alpine lake surrounded by forested hills and charming waterfront villages.
Attend classical music concerts and cultural performances in this renowned arts venue set on a beautiful estate near Magog.
Hike or drive to the summit for panoramic views of Lac Mégantic and the surrounding countryside.
Visit family-owned vineyards and cideries along the scenic Route des Vins, sampling local wines and ciders.
Stroll the promenade along Lac Memphrémagog in Magog's downtown, featuring shops, restaurants, and lake views.
Explore contemporary and classical art collections in Quebec's Eastern Townships cultural hub.
Drive scenic routes through the rolling hills during autumn to witness the spectacular maple leaf colors.

food.detail: Enjoy this iconic dish of fries, gravy, and cheese curds at local diners and casual restaurants throughout the region.
Savor this traditional Quebec French-Canadian savory pie filled with seasoned ground meat, often served at family restaurants.
Sample fresh or mulled apple cider from regional orchards and cideries, especially popular during autumn visits.
Experience this classic Quebec treat of hot maple syrup poured onto clean snow and rolled on a wooden stick at seasonal sugar shacks.
Taste locally brewed craft beers from microbreweries that have grown popular in the Sherbrooke and Magog areas.
Find fine dining restaurants offering regional specialties like duck foie gras and venison sourced from local farms.

culture.detail: While English is spoken in tourist areas, French is the primary language in Eastern Townships; learning basic French phrases is appreciated and helpful.
Plan visits around autumn foliage (September-October) or winter activities, as spring and summer see fewer dramatic changes though remain pleasant for outdoor activities.
The region maintains a warm, small-town atmosphere where locals appreciate genuine interest in their villages and regional heritage.
Many restaurants emphasize locally sourced ingredients and seasonal menus reflecting Québécois agricultural traditions and values.

places.name: Magog
places.description: A charming lakeside town on beautiful Memphrémagog Lake in the Eastern Townships, offering waterfront dining, beaches, and proximity to Mont-Orford provincial park. It's a popular summer and fall destination combining relaxation with outdoor activities in scenic surroundings.

activities.detail: Cool off in the pristine waters of this large cross-border lake with sandy public beaches and designated swimming areas along the waterfront.
Explore scenic trails through forests and mountains just minutes from town, with panoramic views of the lake and surrounding Eastern Townships.
Stroll or bike along the lakeside boardwalk lined with shops, galleries, and parks that showcase Magog's charming downtown core.
Take a guided cruise on the lake to learn about local history and enjoy views of the shoreline and surrounding mountains.

activities.text: Spa and Wellness at Local Resorts
activities.detail: Relax at one of the lakeside spas offering massage, hydrotherapy, and wellness treatments popular among visitors seeking rejuvenation.
Browse independent galleries and artisan shops throughout downtown featuring local pottery, paintings, jewelry, and handcrafted goods.

food.detail: Fresh-caught lake trout prepared simply grilled or pan-seared is a signature dish at local restaurants celebrating the region's fishing heritage.
The Eastern Townships are known for duck farming, and Magog restaurants feature rich pâtés and duck confit dishes highlighting this specialty.
Quebec maple syrup features prominently in desserts, glazes for meats, and traditional maple taffy rolled on clean snow.
Many restaurants emphasize locally-sourced vegetables and meats from nearby Eastern Townships farms, changing with the seasons.
Sample locally-made cheeses and cured meats from regional producers at markets and bistros throughout town.

culture.detail: Magog is in Quebec's Eastern Townships with a mix of French and English speakers; basic French phrases are appreciated though English is widely understood.
September and October bring vibrant autumn colors and significantly higher visitor numbers, so plan accommodations and reservations well in advance.
The town hosts seasonal festivals including the Fête du Lac (summer) and autumn celebrations, so check event schedules to enhance your visit.

culture.text: Respect for Nature and Outdoor Ethics
culture.detail: Leave-no-trace principles and respect for the lake environment are important to locals; stay on marked trails and use designated beaches responsibly.

places.name: Sherbrooke
places.description: The largest city in the Eastern Townships serving as a cultural and educational hub with museums, galleries, and local restaurants in a compact downtown. It offers a more relaxed alternative to Montreal and Quebec City while maintaining good infrastructure and cultural attractions.

activities.detail: Regional art museum housed in a heritage building featuring contemporary and classical Québécois works in an intimate downtown setting.
Linear park along the Magog River offering walking trails, picnic areas, and scenic views perfect for a leisurely afternoon stroll.
Historic 1915 railway station restored as a cultural venue and museum showcasing the region's transportation heritage.
Beautiful hillside campus featuring modern architecture and the stunning Chapelle du Souvenir, open to respectful visitors.

activities.text: Galerie d'art du Conseil des arts
activities.detail: Contemporary art gallery in downtown showcasing local and emerging artists with rotating exhibitions and artist talks.
Hilltop park with a panoramic lookout tower offering 360-degree views of Sherbrooke and the surrounding Appalachian landscape.

food.detail: Traditional Québécois meat pie with a local twist, typically filled with pork and spices, found at local bakeries and restaurants throughout the city.
Fresh local trout sourced from nearby waterways, prepared simply grilled or poached at upscale dining establishments.
Sherbrooke's take on the iconic Québécois dish using fresh local cheese curds squeaking between your teeth, served at casual eateries.
Handmade artisanal chocolates created by a local chocolatier using Belgian techniques and regional ingredients.
Spiced pork spread traditionally enjoyed as a breakfast item or appetizer, reflecting French-Québécois culinary heritage.

culture.detail: While English speakers are accommodated, French is the primary language; learning a few phrases like 'Bonjour' and 'Merci' shows respect and opens doors.
Late spring through early fall hosts numerous festivals including Les Tam-Tams and FEQ Sherbrooke; plan visits around these vibrant community events.
Québécois culture values warm greetings and pleasantries; always begin interactions with a friendly 'Bonjour' or 'Bonsoir' depending on time of day.

culture.text: Local pride in Eastern Townships identity
culture.detail: Sherbrooke residents take pride in their distinct regional culture separate from Montreal and Quebec City; showing interest in local history and traditions is appreciated.

places.name: Laurentian Region
places.description: A vast recreation area north of Montreal featuring lakes, forests, and small villages perfect for cottage country getaways and outdoor adventures. The region offers cottage rentals, water sports, hiking, and a tranquil escape from city life.

activities.detail: North America's largest ski resort by skiable terrain, offering world-class downhill and cross-country skiing in winter and mountain biking in summer.
A pristine lake perfect for paddling, swimming, and spotting wildlife among the quiet forest waters.
Explore over 80 km of trails through boreal forest with views of lakes and mountains, especially stunning during fall foliage season.
Charming pedestrian-friendly village with local shops, galleries, restaurants, and a relaxed cottage-country atmosphere.
The Laurentian lakes are renowned for excellent trout and pike fishing, with many outfitters offering guided trips and equipment rental.
Historic lakeside town with beaches, antique shops, local breweries, and waterfront dining on Lac-des-Sables.

food.detail: A traditional Québécois meat pie typically filled with pork, beef, and spices, served warm at local bakeries and restaurants throughout the region.
Crispy fries topped with gravy and squeaky cheese curds, a beloved Québécois comfort food found at nearly every casual eatery.
Local restaurants serve pan-seared or grilled trout caught from the region's pristine lakes, often prepared simply to highlight the delicate flavor.
A traditional Québécois treat where hot maple syrup is poured onto clean snow, rolled on a stick, and eaten as it cools—a seasonal specialty in winter.
Sainte-Agathe and surrounding villages have excellent artisanal bakeries producing chewy, freshly baked bagels in various flavors.

culture.detail: While English is spoken in tourist areas, learning basic French phrases shows respect and enhances your experience in this francophone region.
The Laurentians are the traditional weekend and summer retreat destination for Montrealers; expect busy periods on weekends and in July-August.

culture.text: Respect quiet hours and lake etiquette
culture.detail: Cottage communities value tranquility, so minimize noise after evening hours and follow local water safety guidelines when boating or swimming.

places.name: Outaouais Region
places.description: A western Quebec region bordering Ontario known for stunning national parks like Gatineau Park with excellent hiking and scenic drives. The region offers cultural attractions, outdoor recreation, and proximity to Ottawa making it ideal for nature lovers and active travelers.

activities.detail: Explore over 361 square kilometers of forested parkland with more than 90 kilometers of trails ranging from easy walks to challenging hikes with panoramic Ottawa Valley views.
This popular lakeside destination offers a sandy beach, picnic areas, and water activities including swimming and paddling in the summer months.
Located in Gatineau, this world-class museum showcases Canadian heritage through interactive exhibits spanning Indigenous cultures, colonial periods, and contemporary society.
A picturesque drive around Meech Lake offers forest views, historical significance related to constitutional negotiations, and access to several hiking trails and picnic spots.
Discover Gatineau's former working-class neighborhood with heritage architecture, local galleries, artisan shops, and waterfront promenades along the Ottawa River.
A quieter alternative to Lac-Leamy, this beach offers swimming, kayaking, and surrounded by dense forest perfect for nature photography and peaceful retreats.

food.detail: A traditional French-Canadian pastry filled with seasoned ground pork and spices, commonly served during holidays and available at local bakeries and restaurants throughout Outaouais.
A layered casserole of ground meat, corn, and mashed potatoes that represents Quebec comfort food tradition and is featured in family-style restaurants across the region.
Outaouais producers craft ciders from regional apples and meads from local honey, available at farmers markets and specialty shops reflecting the region's agricultural heritage.
Fresh-smoked trout, salmon, and whitefish sourced from Ottawa River and regional waters are prepared by local smoking houses and sold at markets.
A beloved Québécois dessert of sponge cake topped with hot caramel sauce, served warm and found in traditional restaurants honoring Quebec's culinary heritage.

culture.detail: Outaouais is predominantly French-speaking with English widely spoken due to proximity to Ottawa; visitors should expect bilingual signage and appreciate the region's Franco-Ontarian and Québécois cultures.
Gatineau Park has specific operating hours and seasonal closures for vehicle traffic; check ahead before visiting and respect designated wildlife protection areas.
The region is traditional territory of the Algonquin Anishinaabe people; many local sites and museums honor this heritage, and visitors should approach cultural spaces with respect and awareness.

restaurants.name: Toqué!
restaurants.description: One of Canada's most acclaimed fine dining restaurants offering innovative seasonal cuisine with impeccable service.
restaurants.mustOrder: Chef's tasting menu with wine pairings

restaurants.name: Joe Beef
restaurants.description: Legendary casual fine dining spot known for premium cuts, fresh oysters, and an exceptional wine list in a vintage setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Dry-aged steak or whole roasted lobster

restaurants.name: Maison Boulud
restaurants.description: Daniel Boulud's flagship Montreal restaurant delivering refined French gastronomy in an elegant historic mansion.
restaurants.mustOrder: Foie gras terrine or diver scallops

restaurants.name: L'Aromaterie
restaurants.description: Intimate neighborhood bistro showcasing creative French cuisine with seasonal ingredients in a warm, unpretentious atmosphere.
restaurants.mustOrder: Duck confit or fresh pasta specials

restaurants.name: Cabaret Lion d'Or
restaurants.description: Trendy brasserie combining playful French classics with local Québécois ingredients in a lively, vibrant dining room.
restaurants.mustOrder: Pâté en croûte or escargot cassoulet

restaurants.name: Au Pied de Cochon
restaurants.description: Iconic poutine and comfort food restaurant celebrating traditional Québécois cuisine with refined technique and joie de vivre.
restaurants.mustOrder: Foie gras poutine or lamb tongue

restaurants.name: Charcuterie Centrale
restaurants.description: Artisanal Italian salumi and cured meat specialist offering sandwiches and small plates in a rustic, casual setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: House-made charcuterie board or mortadella sandwich

restaurants.name: Nora Gray
restaurants.description: Minimalist Italian pasta restaurant in a converted warehouse producing handmade pasta daily with impeccable ingredients.

restaurants.name: Les Enfants Terribles
restaurants.description: Lively neighborhood bistro serving classic French comfort food with an excellent wine selection at reasonable prices.
restaurants.mustOrder: Steak frites or coq au vin

restaurants.name: Witchcraft
restaurants.description: Casual, popular brunch spot and cafe known for creative breakfast dishes, quality coffee, and a bohemian neighborhood vibe.

restaurants.name: Rôtisserie Laurier BBQ
restaurants.description: No-frills rotisserie serving authentic Portuguese-style grilled chicken and meats with classic sides at excellent value.
restaurants.mustOrder: Half chicken with piri piri sauce

restaurants.name: Restaurant Initiale
restaurants.description: Elegant fine dining establishment in historic Old Quebec offering refined French cuisine with impeccable technique and service.
restaurants.mustOrder: Chef's tasting menu or local seafood preparations

restaurants.name: Le Champlain
restaurants.description: Historic restaurant with river views specializing in traditional Québécois and French cuisine in an elegant, romantic setting.

restaurants.name: L'Entrecôte Saint-Jean
restaurants.description: Classic Parisian-style bistro serving steak frites and traditional French fare in a cozy, welcoming neighborhood location.
